items.title: Cary Town Council holds first work session since release of state audit
items.summary_raw: Cary leaders gathered on Tuesday for their first work session on town affairs since the State Auditor's Office released a new report on financial mismanagement in the town.

items.title: Lightning topples tree, igniting natural gas line near Harnett County home
items.summary_raw: Piedmont Natural Gas said crews were able to safely extinguish the line before any further damage was done.

items.title: Smithsonian too focused on 'oppressors and the oppressed,' say House Republicans
items.summary_raw: In a House DOGE subcommittee hearing, GOP representatives excoriated the director of the National Museum of American History, following a White House report earlier this month that also criticized her.

items.title: Zelensky sacks Ukraine's top army commander after days of protests
items.summary_raw: Oleksandr Syrskyi is dismissed after backlash over the removal of popular Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov.

items.title: Watching too much TV in midlife may shrink the brain
items.summary_raw: Watching television very often in midlife may be linked to troubling brain changes decades later. In a study of about 1,700 adults, frequent TV viewers later showed smaller brain regions involved in memory, decision-making, and visual processing, along with more signs of damage in the brain’s white matter. Surprisingly, sitting itself did not appear to be the main issue, since people who spent long hours seated at mentally engaging jobs often showed healthier brain patterns.

items.title: Fact-check: Does Elon Musk pay the same amount into Social Security as someone making $185K?
items.summary_raw: “Today, Elon Musk, a trillionaire, pays the same amount into Social Security as someone making $184,500,” Sen. Bernie Sanders, I-Vt., said in a June 12 social media post, calling the situation absurd and in need of reform.

items.title: Ask the Meteorologist: Why do storms sound louder at night?
items.summary_raw: It's partially our mind playing tricks, and it's partially atmospheric science. Storms can sound louder at night!

items.title: OpenAI Models Escaped Containment and Hacked Hugging Face
items.summary_raw: The cybersecurity-focused models, including GPT-5.6 Sol, broke out of a testing sandbox, exploited a zero-day, and gained access to the open internet to pull off the attack.

items.title: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth goes to the Hill with nearly $90B funding request
items.summary_raw: Defense secretary Pete Hegseth will appear on the Hill on Tuesday. He's looking for a nearly $90 billion funding boost, as the Iran war once again flares.

items.title: Colorado wildfire victims return to their homes for the first time
items.summary_raw: Colorado's biggest wildfire this year has destroyed more than 300 homes. People who lost them are just now being allowed back to their properties.

items.title: Lebanese army says troops deploying in 'pilot zone' after Israeli withdrawal
items.summary_raw: A US-brokered agreement should see Israeli troops pulling out of a small part of Lebanon occupied during their conflict with Hezbollah.

items.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari as world’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
items.summary_raw: Ancient sediment reveals that some of K’gari’s deepest lakes vanished around 7,500 years ago, despite unusually heavy rainfall. The surprising discovery suggests changing winds and future climate shifts could threaten the sacred lakes known as the Eyes of K’gari.

items.title: Chatham County woman charged after dozens of animals allegedly abandoned
items.summary_raw: A Siler City woman is facing dozens of charges after investigators say she left multiple animals without proper care.

items.title: Ask the Meteorologist: How does current storm in the Gulf differ from Bertha of 1996?
items.summary_raw: A tropical depression in the Gulf will soon become Tropical Storm Bertha. How does this storm differ for us from what Hurricane Bertha brought in 1996?

items.title: Shop Best Buy’s Black Friday in July Sale 2026 Before Fall Chaos
items.summary_raw: The discounts may not beat Prime Day, but I found worthwhile prices on WIRED-approved TVs, laptops, tablets, speakers, and other home gear.

items.title: Sen. Chris Murphy on the Iran war and Pete Hegseth's upcoming Senate testimony
items.summary_raw: NPR's Michel Martin speaks with Sen. Chris Murphy, D-Conn., about the escalating war in the Middle East and the testimony of Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth in front of a Senate panel on Tuesday.

items.title: Man accused of setting a fire outside a federal building in New York City
items.summary_raw: Authorities detained a former U.S. Army soldier who is accused of starting a fire and shooting a pellet gun outside of a federal building in New York City that houses an immigration court.

items.title: Carney looking at 'all options' as Trump announces 50% tariffs on Canada
items.summary_raw: The duties mark a major escalation in trade tensions between the North American neighbours.

items.title: China’s Chang’e-6 reveals why solar wind hits the Moon’s two sides differently
items.summary_raw: China’s Chang’e-6 samples have uncovered a surprising difference between the Moon’s two hemispheres. Solar wind particles penetrated deeper into the far-side soil because Earth’s magnetosphere slows the particles that reach the near side. Noble gases locked inside the lunar regolith preserved evidence of this uneven bombardment. These gases may even help scientists reconstruct the ancient history of Earth’s magnetic shield.

items.title: Woman arrested after $35K in thefts from NC Lululemon, Ulta stores
items.summary_raw: Cary police allege two women stole more than $35,000 in clothing, cosmetics and fragrances from retailers across the Triangle, and obtained a warrant to search a phone for communications and evidence connecting the incidents.

items.title: Taylor Farms Spent Big on MAGA and Anti-Regulatory Lobbying Before Diarrhea Outbreak
items.summary_raw: The company donated more than $3.6 million to conservative groups between 2020 and 2025, including $1 million to the MAGA Inc. super PAC.

items.title: Trump imposes 50% tariffs on Canadian goods
items.summary_raw: President Donald Trump on Monday imposed 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods, declaring that Canada has unfairly discriminated against American autos, alcohol and dairy products.

items.summary_raw: President Donald Trump on Monday imposed 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods, declaring that Canada has unfairly discriminated against American autos, alcohol and dairy products.

items.title: Two French firefighters die tackling blaze near Bordeaux airport
items.summary_raw: The blaze close to the parking lots at Bordeaux-Mérignac Airport is said to have originated from a brush fire.

items.title: Astronomers find the first atmosphere on a rocky world in the habitable zone
items.summary_raw: Astronomers have found the first confirmed atmosphere around a rocky planet in another star’s habitable zone. The planet, LHS 1140 b, revealed its atmosphere through helium slowly leaking into space. Located 48 light-years away, the world may have preserved its atmosphere for billions of years, making it a promising target in the search for potentially habitable planets.

items.title: Headed to see Guns N' Roses or Noah Kahan this weekend? Here's what you need to know before you go
items.summary_raw: Carter-Finley Stadium is hosting big concerts this weekend! Guns N' Roses kicks it off on Thursday, while Noah Kahan performs Saturday.

items.summary_raw: Pat Gelsinger wants to pave the way to ever more powerful artificial intelligence using tiny beams of light.

items.title: Arizona primaries set the stage for contested fall races in a swing state
items.summary_raw: Arizona Democrats choose who can try to flip a Republican-held House seat, and Republicans pick their nominee to try to unseat a Democratic governor.

items.title: How soccer brings a Bhutanese community together in Harrisburg, Pa.
items.summary_raw: Harrisburg is home to one of the nation's largest communities of Nepali-speaking Bhutanese refugees and their families. Through soccer, generations have found connection, belonging and a way to carry forward a shared history.

items.title: US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers
items.summary_raw: Iran says it has hit two ships in the Strait of Hormuz as well as targets in Bahrain and Jordan.

items.title: Ancient DNA reveals a chilling secret at China’s 4,000-year-old Shimao city
items.summary_raw: Ancient DNA from China’s vast 4,000-year-old Shimao settlement is revealing how one of East Asia’s earliest complex societies was organized. Researchers found that its people were largely descended from local Yellow River farming communities, but also had genetic ties to distant southern and steppe populations, pointing to extensive prehistoric contact.

items.title: Hat trick: Another Peace Street Bridge truck collision is third in a week
items.summary_raw: Call it a hat trick. This is the third truck to collide with Raleigh's Peace Street bridge in a week's time.

items.title: A Sneaky Hacking Tool Targeting AI Infrastructure Is Lurking in Victims’ Blind Spots
items.summary_raw: A new type of malware can worm deep into AI coding systems to steal data and logins—and can flip a “death switch” to destroy files and keep out real users.

items.title: New Air Force One going out of commission to get safety upgrades
items.summary_raw: The new Air Force One is going out of commission to receive safety upgrades. What needs to be done and was it safe for President Trump to fly the plane to Turkey earlier this month?

items.title: Amid nurse shortage, a university rolls out the welcome mat for men
items.summary_raw: Only about 1 in 8 nurses are men, but their share is growing. A strong job market for nurses and sustained efforts to recruit men into the field have made a difference, including in the South.

items.title: Brazil's Amazon sees sharp decline in size of area burned in 2025
items.summary_raw: The amount of land burned in the world's largest rainforest was the smallest since MapBiomas records began in 1985.

items.title: A hidden “switch-off” signal could predict solar storms seven years early
items.summary_raw: A newly discovered turning point in the Sun’s 11-year cycle could allow scientists to predict future solar activity years earlier than before. At this “switch-off” point, the most violent space weather abruptly ends, and the remaining number of sunspots offers clues about the next cycle’s strength. Early projections suggest Solar Cycle 26 may be moderate, but a clearer forecast is expected in about two years.

items.title: Fallen airman gets honor escort on return to Goldsboro
items.summary_raw: The NC Patriot Guard on Tuesday escorted the body of Maj. Alexander Davis home to Goldsboro. Davis was one of eight crewmembers killed after a B-52 crashed in June.

items.title: The Light Flip Is the Stylish Dumb Flip Phone of Your Dreams
items.summary_raw: A flip phone with 5G, USB-C, a headphone jack, and a removable battery? Say it ain’t so.

items.title: With just a week left until August recess, Congress faces long to-do list
items.summary_raw: Before the House adjourns for its August recess, House Speaker Mike Johnson wants to advance more defense funding, the president's election integrity bill and a stock trading ban for lawmakers.

items.summary_raw: Arizona Democrats choose who can try to flip a Republican-held House seat, and Republicans pick their nominee to try to unseat a Democratic governor.

items.summary_raw: Firas al-Masri, his wife and their four children died in one attack, which the Israeli military says targeted a Hamas operative.

items.title: Scientists found mystery bacteria inside one of the ocean’s most elusive whales
items.summary_raw: Scientists studying decades of pygmy sperm whale strandings have uncovered three previously unknown types of Helicobacter bacteria hiding in the animals’ stomachs. All four infected whales showed serious digestive damage, including inflammation, ulcers, scarring, and parasite infestations, although the bacteria were not confirmed as the cause of death.

items.title: Diarrhea-causing bug reaches outbreak proportions in NC with 500+ cases
items.summary_raw: State health officials have confirmed North Carolina is experiencing a Cyclospora outbreak after reporting 561 cases since May, as of July 21, 2026.

items.title: Nvidia Wants to Own Every Chip Inside AI Data Centers
items.summary_raw: Nvidia’s Vera Rubin platform combines CPUs and GPUs into a single system, reflecting the company’s growing ambition to power every layer of AI infrastructure.

items.title: Republican strategist on the hurdles the GOP faces to pass reconciliation bill
items.summary_raw: NPR's Leila Fadel speaks with Republican strategist Rob Burgess about Republican efforts to move forward with a $95 billion party-line budget reconciliation bill.

items.title: A homeless man was charged with a felony for camping. He's one of the first
items.summary_raw: When Joseph Lamont Williams refused to leave the public park where he has lived for years, he was charged with a felony. Camping bans are on the rise as states seek to crack down on homelessness.

items.title: Watch Texas police officer pull man from burning car
items.summary_raw: Bodycam and dashcam footage shows a police officer rescue a man seconds before his car was engulfed in flames in Texas.

items.title: New molecular map reveals how the flu virus hijacks human cells
items.summary_raw: Scientists uncovered how influenza A commandeers human cells, including a surprising strategy that dissolves tiny structures in the nucleus and releases proteins the virus may use to reproduce. The detailed molecular map could reveal new drug targets and help researchers study dangerous flu strains such as H5N1.

items.title: Falls Lake rises another 1% as recent rainfall boosts water levels
items.summary_raw: Falls Lake continues to recover as recent rainfall pushes the reservoir to 66% full. While the region remains in a drought, the lake has gained four percentage points over the past two weeks.

items.title: The Great Peptide Cash Grab Has Begun
items.summary_raw: The FDA could decide whether to loosen rules around peptide production this week. Some telehealth players are already seeing dollar signs.

items.title: Maine Democrats close in on pick to replace Senate candidate Graham Platner
items.summary_raw: Maine Democrats appear to be settling on a frontrunner to replace former Senate candidate Graham Platner in a rushed process to take on Republican Sen. Susan Collins.

items.title: Colorado wildfire victims return to their homes for the first time
items.summary_raw: Colorado's biggest wildfire this year has destroyed more than 300 homes. People who lost them are just now being allowed back to their properties.

items.title: Dozens hurt in Bologna protests after man dies while being restrained by police
items.summary_raw: Thousands of people took to the streets demanding justice for a Moroccan-born man who died on Sunday.

items.title: This food compound may help vaccines work better in older adults
items.summary_raw: A naturally occurring compound found in foods such as wheat germ, mushrooms, and aged cheese may help older adults build stronger protection after vaccination. In a small pilot trial, researchers gave spermidine supplements to adults over 65 following their third COVID-19 vaccine dose. The biggest improvements appeared among people whose immune systems initially responded poorly, with higher antibody levels and stronger activity against several variants.

items.title: Second Fort Bragg soldier killed in action in Iraq identified: 'He loved his wife and two children'
items.summary_raw: Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, of Fayetteville, North Carolina, was killed in action during a controlled detonation of ordinance from a downed Iranian drone.

items.title: Halliday’s New Smart Glasses Skip the Camera
items.summary_raw: Halliday’s G2 glasses can listen to and summarize your workplace meetings—with no video involved.

items.title: Georgia official discusses Trump's claims about election security
items.summary_raw: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe talks with Gabriel Sterling, of the Georgia Secretary of State's Office, about President Trump's claims about election security.

items.title: Civil rights leaders call for justice at funeral for Mississippi teen
items.summary_raw: Civil rights leaders called for justice Monday at the funeral of Nolan Wells, the 18-year-old who was found dead on Horn Island, Miss. He was with friends for Independence Day and did not return home.

items.title: Shock and anger after top Ghanaian politician sentenced to 20 years for illegal mining
items.summary_raw: Bernard Antwi Boasiako, known as "Chairman Wontumi", was also fined more than $10,000 (£7,450).

items.title: Dying Sun-like stars may kick themselves through space
items.summary_raw: Dying Sun-like stars may not fade away quietly. As blobs of gas erupt unevenly from their swollen surfaces, each burst gives the star a tiny push in the opposite direction. Thousands of these random kicks could eventually break apart distant stellar pairs or, in rare cases, drive two stars into a violent collision.

items.title: Holly Springs officer honored for pulling 11-year-old boy from icy pond
items.summary_raw: A Holly Springs police officer will be honored on Tuesday for his heroic actions in January.

items.title: 8 Best Smartwatches (2026): Apple, Google, and Hybrid Watches
items.summary_raw: These WIRED-tested wearables reduce your reliance on a phone while keeping you connected.

items.title: Police officers arrested for allegedly seeking roadside bribe from Nigeria's anti-corruption boss
items.summary_raw: They are accused of stopping Musa Aliyu at a fake roadblock and telling him to drive to a cash machine.

items.title: New quantum gravity theory links entropy, dark energy, and life
items.summary_raw: A new theoretical study offers a possible explanation for how the Universe can grow more complex without violating the second law of thermodynamics. Using a quantum gravity framework called Gravity from Entropy, mathematician Ginestra Bianconi found that the Universe’s total entropy may rise as space expands, even while entropy within each unit of volume falls. That decline could leave room for galaxies, stars, planets, and life to form locally amid an overall increase in cosmic entropy.

items.title: Special agents raid, shut down Triangle business tied to recent violence
items.summary_raw: Agents say the raid was part of a larger operation cracking down on criminal activity associated with licensed and illegal alcohol outlets.

items.title: The Best Smart Home Accessories to Boost Your Curb Appeal (2026)
items.summary_raw: These locks, lights, and other smart home upgrades let you add automation without messing up your home’s vibe.

items.title: Watch: Flooded towns and evacuations as Chile hit by heavy rain
items.summary_raw: A "state of catastrophe" has been declared in Coquimbo and Huasco following the downpour.

items.title: New antibody drug stops aggressive prostate cancer from spreading
items.summary_raw: Scientists have developed a fully human antibody that halted the growth and spread of aggressive prostate cancer in preclinical research. Although more testing is needed, the drug’s unusual mechanism could open the door to a safer new treatment for advanced cancer.

items.title: Abdul El-Sayed Is an Epidemiologist Running for Senate. His State Is a Public Health Disaster
items.summary_raw: Michigan has been at the epicenter of the wildfire smoke and explosive diarrhea outbreaks. The progressive Democrat talks to WIRED about how he would tackle the forces behind them if he’s elected.

items.title: Why are thousands protesting for education reform in India?
items.summary_raw: Thousands of mostly young people tried to march on India's parliament to demand changes to how the education system is run.

items.title: Scientists detect hidden skin damage before it becomes visible
items.summary_raw: Scientists have discovered a hidden warning sign of skin damage that appears before collagen fibers visibly thin, break, or lose their connections. Using advanced light-based techniques, the team found that collagen’s carefully organized molecular “handedness” can collapse even while the tissue still appears structurally intact and contains the same amount of collagen. This suggests that skin may begin deteriorating at a much deeper level long before conventional imaging can detect a problem.

items.title: ‘It’s a Modern-Day Draft’: Why Stanford Students Walked Out on Sundar Pichai’s Commencement Speech
items.summary_raw: Last month, more than a hundred Stanford students left their own graduation to protest Google’s military contracts and deals with ICE. Two organizers, Amanda Campos and Eva Jones, tell us why.

items.title: India's 'cockroach' protest continues a day after police crackdown
items.summary_raw: Delhi witnessed overwhelming violence on Monday, with at least 60 protesters wounded. Police said 118 of their men were also injured.

items.title: Some dinosaurs could rise like giants until they grew too big
items.summary_raw: Two elephant-sized South American sauropods were unusually well built for standing on their hind legs. Digital tests showed that their robust femurs handled the enormous forces better than those of larger species, especially while the dinosaurs were young. The upright pose may have helped them reach treetops, frighten predators, or impress potential mates.

items.title: Stop Saying Kids Can’t Read
items.summary_raw: Everyone is talking about the reading crisis. But inside the “Mississippi Miracle,” it’s all about comprehension.

items.title: 'Anti-ICE' army veteran started fire outside Manhattan federal building, say officials
items.summary_raw: Andrew Arrabaca, 43, was armed with two axes, a machete, three knives and fireworks, says the FBI.

items.title: Earth’s waters are quietly running out of oxygen, scientists warn
items.summary_raw: Oxygen is disappearing from oceans, lakes, rivers, and coastal waters at an alarming rate, threatening aquatic life and weakening the natural processes that help regulate Earth’s climate. Scientists warn that this widespread deoxygenation is closely connected to other major planetary threats, including warming, pollution, biodiversity loss, and ocean acidification.
